responses.text: The Atlas Obscura article by Dan Nosowitz, originally published June 4, 2015 and updated August 19, 2020, asserts that Hawaiian pizza was invented in 1962 by Greek-Canadian restaurateur Sam Panopoulos in Chatham, Ontario, not in Hawaii.

This central origin claim is well corroborated by independent reporting: the BBC, CBC, NBC News, and the Canadian Encyclopedia all credit Sam Panopoulos and his Satellite Restaurant in Chatham, Ontario with creating Hawaiian pizza in 1962.

Atlas Obscura is a generally reliable feature outlet for food and travel history, but its Gastro Obscura pieces are narrative features rather than investigative reporting and rely heavily on a single primary source — Panopoulos himself in a phone interview — so secondary corroboration matters.

The article's framing that Panopoulos got the idea after being inspired by sweet-and-sour Chinese flavors and tossed canned pineapple onto a pizza on a whim is consistent with Panopoulos's own later interviews with the BBC and CBC, in which he gave the same explanation.

One detail is incomplete: Atlas Obscura implies Panopoulos settled directly in Chatham after arriving in Canada in 1954, but the Canadian Encyclopedia and Canada's History report that he first worked in mines in Sudbury and Elliot Lake before moving to southwestern Ontario and eventually opening the Satellite Restaurant with his brothers.

The article suggests Panopoulos chose the name 'Hawaiian' to evoke tiki and island culture, but the BBC, the Canadian Encyclopedia, and other reports specify that the pizza was named after the 'Hawaiian' brand printed on the can of pineapple he used, a notable piece of missing context.
The article's note that Panopoulos died on June 8, 2017 at age 83 is accurate and matches obituaries and news reports from the BBC, CBC, and NBC News.

Background claims in the article check out: Hawaii became the 50th U.S. state on August 21, 1959, and the Margherita pizza is traditionally said to have been created in Naples in 1889 in honor of Queen Margherita of Savoy, although food historians note the 1889 origin story is part legend.

The article's claim that the New York Times first introduced pizza to its readers in 1944 is broadly consistent with widely cited NYT food coverage from that era, though the dish had appeared in earlier U.S. publications and Italian-American communities had eaten it for decades prior, so the NYT mention is a milestone of mainstream attention rather than the dish's true U.S. debut.

Beyond the Atlas Obscura piece, no credible counter-source disputes Panopoulos's authorship of Hawaiian pizza; while some commentators have joked about alternative origins, no documented competing claim has been substantiated in mainstream reporting.

Bottom line: the article's core factual claims — that Hawaiian pizza was invented in 1962 by Sam Panopoulos at the Satellite Restaurant in Chatham, Ontario, using canned pineapple and ham — are accurate and independently corroborated, while a few peripheral details (Panopoulos's pre-Chatham years and the precise reason for the 'Hawaiian' name) are simplified or slightly misleading and warrant minor caveats.
